title: Feature-Diversity-Aware Supervised Contrastive Learning: Mitigating Feature Suppression through Adaptive Pair Weighting
description: We propose FD-SCL, a novel supervised contrastive learning method that addresses feature suppression (class collapse) by adaptively weighting positive pairs based on their feature diversity contribution. A lightweight Feature Diversity Estimation Module (FDEM) estimates feature rarity from batch statistics and upweights pairs sharing rare features, preventing the model from focusing only on dominant class features. FD-SCL learns more comprehensive representations with minimal computational overhead (<5%) compared to multi-stage alternatives.
motivation: Supervised Contrastive Learning (SCL) suffers from feature suppression: models learn only a subset of discriminative features within each class while suppressing diverse features. This 'class collapse' phenomenon significantly compromises representation quality for fine-grained tasks. Existing solutions like Multistage Contrastive Learning (MCL) require training the model 2-3 times, incurring high computational cost. We need an efficient, single-stage solution that preserves feature diversity without auxiliary labels or synthetic data.
proposed_approach: FD-SCL introduces a Feature Diversity Estimation Module (FDEM) that: (1) computes feature activation statistics across the batch, (2) estimates pairwise feature rarity scores based on shared active dimensions, and (3) converts these to diversity-aware weights via softmax normalization. The weighted SCL loss then upweights positive pairs that share rare features, preventing their suppression. The method requires no additional labels, adds <5% computational overhead, and integrates seamlessly with existing SCL frameworks.

related_work.title: Supervised Contrastive Learning
related_work.key_difference: Standard SCL weights all positive pairs equally, causing feature suppression. FD-SCL adaptively weights pairs based on feature diversity.

related_work.title: Which Features are Learnt by Contrastive Learning? On the Role of Simplicity Bias in Class Collapse and Feature Suppression
related_work.key_difference: Provides theoretical analysis of feature suppression but suggests increasing embedding dimension as solution. FD-SCL directly addresses the root cause through adaptive weighting.

related_work.title: Learning the Unlearned: Mitigating Feature Suppression in Contrastive Learning
related_work.key_difference: Proposes MCL with multi-stage training (2-3× compute). FD-SCL achieves similar benefits in a single stage with minimal overhead.

related_work.title: Adaptive Multi-head Contrastive Learning
related_work.key_difference: Uses multiple projection heads with adaptive temperatures. FD-SCL uses single head with pair-wise diversity weighting, addressing feature suppression more directly.

related_work.title: Hard-Negative Sampling for Contrastive Learning
related_work.key_difference: Focuses on hard negative selection. FD-SCL focuses on positive pair diversity within classes.

hypothesis: Weighting positive pairs in supervised contrastive learning based on their feature diversity contribution—upweighting pairs sharing rare features—will reduce feature suppression, improve subclass discrimination, and yield better fine-grained recognition without requiring multi-stage training or additional supervision.

primary: At least 5 percentage points higher subclass accuracy than SCL when trained with superclass labels (CIFAR-100 coarse/fine)
At least 2 percentage points improvement over SCL on fine-grained datasets (FGVC-Aircraft, Stanford Cars)
Effective rank of representations at least 15% higher than SCL
Less than 10% training overhead compared to SCL
secondary: Maintain or improve standard classification accuracy
